speaker: interviewer
text: Sydney, Australia. Elizabeth, you're telling us about going to Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was-- we went to Vienna. But in the border, the police catch us and the Russians. And they-- they wanted to put us back to Hungary. But then-- then-- but when-- when we crossed the border, the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the man who was paid to bring us over there-- but I don't tell you how we get to Pozsony. You know, we-- we-- that man said to me to go and get a ticket to the train and put the child on the train, and you go down and only when the-- when the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Train starts to move.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moved. Then you go up. So can you imagine what I feel? I put my little girl on the train and leave her there. And the train started to move. And then he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Signalled.

speaker: interviewee
text: Signalled. And then we go up. And we get-- and then we go to the little girl. And she was sitting alone. She was one year old, and she was sitting alone. And don't say a word, because she doesn't-- only speak Hungarian. So if she-- she speak, they'd know it straight away. And then [? Apu ?] come to me and said, look, we have to separate, because one man watching me. So when we went over the train--

speaker: interviewer
text: At Pozsony.

speaker: interviewee
text: On Pozsony-- he said, I meet you in a-- in a Jewish kitchen. There was a Jewish kitchen in Pozsony. But I getting there? You know? And I take my little girl. And we don't speak the language. So-- but we take a taxi and said the Jewish kitchen. That is everywhere the same word.

speaker: interviewer
text: [HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he took us there. And I  have some money. So I gave it to him. And when we come out in the Jewish kitchen, my husband coming off the train in the same time. He come in a different way. But we meet. But we can lost each other so easily. But God was with us, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Pozsony you went to Vienna? And we went to Vienna again on the border. And come in the police, and they shoot. And they want to shoot the men, or [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], who was taking with us. And I was-- The smuggler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the smuggler. And because before we take that trip out of Hungary, I went to the clairvoyant. And he said to me, you are going far away. But look after your husband, because I see shots. And when the police shoot-- want to shoot at smuggler, I was looking for my husband, because I remember what they said. And thanks God, it was nothing. But he was gone again. And he take our money again, [? and they ?] [? gained ?] [? us ?] money. But we were hoping because we paid some money that we will get it in Vienna. And they take us to the jail, because we-- without--

speaker: interviewer
text: Papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: --papers we crossed the border. So it was Christmas Day. And Christmas Day was always very, very important in my life. And they took me with the little girl to the jail. And all the prostitutes who was there, they brought her chocolate and some cakes. They love her because she was a very sweet little baba. And for one day, I was separated from my husband. He went to the other jail. And we went to the other jail. And then the-- the-- the Jewish people, they they come and get us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take me to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lager.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lager, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sewing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sewing. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a sewing machine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I bought it with somebody. Because we paid some money in Budapest, we're getting in the [NON-ENGLISH] Strasse, 83. And we went there. And it was a store. Nobody lived there. So we lost our money. So we was penniless.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my friend was there who has money from my childhood. And he lent me 20 American dollars. And I buy a sewing machine with a foot. And I started to make a pretty girl's-- pretty frocks, to the Joint-- give them to the people. And everybody wanted to look nice. So I make it nice for them. And I started to earn money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lived 10 of us in one room-- 10 people in one room. But I scrubbed it and cleaned it. It was always clean, because my little girl was walking in the-- you know, in the floor. And my husband got a job. He made gloves. So we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what your plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We want to go to Israel, of course. And  we were staying there for two years. And nothing happened because the Jewish people-- you know, they don't let you die. But they don't let you live sometimes. And they take first-- the Polish people come first. So they always put the Polish people to the transport to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sitting there and waiting and waiting. And then, we talk it over. And I said to my darling husband-- I said, look [? Apu, ?] we have to go somewhere. We can't sit here forever. So we go to the Australian consulate. Why Australian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because America, you can't go. You need a quota. And you do that. And you need that, you know? So and the Australia-- it look nice to me. You know, it's  a peaceful, far away from everywhere. I said to him, maybe we go to Australia. And [? Apu ?] signed a contract to the [INAUDIBLE]. And when we have the papers and everything to go, then they said-- the Israeli Consul came and said, now you can go to Israel. I said, no, I don't go anymore. I had enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did come to Australia. And I'm happy to come to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you came to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you come to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Bonegilla first. We went to that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: A migrant camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Migrant camp, yes. And then my husband got a job in the PHP to Newcastle.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the steel works.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was cleaning the big fire where they are cooking the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The furnaces.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then a few weeks after, he sent for me, you know. And I go with my little girl to Newcastle. And I started to work. I take my little girl's hand. And [INAUDIBLE] I don't speak English-- very, very, very little. And I go house by house. And I said I can sew. And by hand, I make up the hand. And we make it in. And I get a cup of tea and a half a dozen lemons. And-- and my little girl had a sandwich. And we go home in the nighttime. And we already started to work. And then my friends find here a boarding house. We come to Sydney. I come first to Sydney. And Daddy stayed over there, because he can't come. If you haven't got a-- a home, place to stay, you can't come out of the camp. So he-- I come first. And I live in the boarding house. And I-- I get a job straightaway going to the blouse manufacturing. They speak Hungarian. So-- And I get the job. And my little girl went to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kindergarten. But she doesn't speak English. So she speaks German because she went to the German kindergarten. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started to work. I take some home work with that machine. I bring out that machine. I pay for that. Every weekend-- every Shabbat-- I can work in the-- in the lager. And we go to my friend's place. And I take my money. And I pay off the $20 that they lent to me. When we came to Australia, I had no debts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to live in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband joined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. After three weeks, he can come.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had another child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not then, you know, before first, we wanted to build up our life. So after five years, we could afford to have Shimon, Peter, and then I had a stillborn child after that. And Shimon is now 40 years. The other little boy should be 38 if he is alive. But no more children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked hard in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a blouse factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First, I was working making makeup for that blouse manufacturer. And then I-- I start my business. And [LAUGHS] we still didn't speak very well English. So-- and my husband lost his job. He was working in a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] handbag factory. And he lost his job. And I said that God wanted it that way. And now, we are working together. So we started to  work together. I make a [INAUDIBLE] lovely samples. And he don't want to-- oh, my darling husband. He don't-- he was ashamed he didn't speak well English. And I was standing in the door. And I sent him in. And I was waiting outside in the door. And he don't want to-- he can't come out. He had to sell it. And he sell it. That was the first business. Next to [INAUDIBLE] was a lingerie business and a little shop. And they liked my things. And they buy it. And I got my first store then. And I was the happiest woman in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Day and night-- day and night. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And, unfortunately, your husband passed away in 1990. But you have beautiful grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [SOBBING] but it's not fair. It's not fair because now I need him. And we can really live our life how we should. But we don't have it when we were young. Now, he has to go. And I can't get over it. We was happily married for 45 years. And I miss him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elizabeth, you survived despite all the hardship that you went through, all the experiences. And your family didn't survive. Why did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I always said that God must have something to-- to-- I should prove to him, you know, because he-- now, my-- when my dear husband is gone, but children need me. And that's the only thing which is keeping me here, because all the people who was close to me and whom I love is over on the other side. And I am waiting to go there. But I think my children still need me. That's why [INAUDIBLE] leave me here-- because they need me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've got four beautiful grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to meet them in a minute. But before we do, would you have a message for your children and grandchildren, a message that you would give them about what has happened about the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: To never forget what's happened. Never forget-- forgive, but don't forget, like I do. I-- I forgive, but never forget. And this shouldn't happen again, because it's-- it's-- I can't find the word-- word for it, because it's how can people so cruel, and take the other people's life, and-- and take them in pieces, and throw them everywhere? Why? What I have done to deserve what I have? What I done? I was not so bad girl. I was polite with everybody how they said-- how my mother taught me. So I don't know why I deserved that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you met some very good people, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. That priest-- God bless him where he is. I going home back to Hungary sometimes. I always go to see his grave because--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you've got his Bible, haven't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, not a Bible. That's the prayer book. You know, the priests have to pray every morning, day, and nighttime. And the housekeeper, before she died, she knows how close we was together. She sent it to me to have his prayer book, because he was very proud of me because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did my best to please him in the way how he wanted in his religion and everything, every way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And being a Jew today--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at the end of-- the end of the 20th century, how do you feel about what has happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel it's-- it's not fair about what's happened. And my-- when my darling husband died, the children wanted to take him to the North Shore Cemetery. And I said, no, because we have here my mother's memory to that cemetery. And I wanted to be close to her to-- to, you know-- to that memory to [? visit. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which cemetery?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Rockwood, because she's there. And I will be there. And then the family is together. At last, we will be together. We will be together. But it's not-- not fair what was happening. It's-- it's-- I don't find the answer-- why? Why? What we do wrong? What we do wrong? We was not killers. We was not cheaters. We-- we live a normal life. We don't deserve it. We don't deserve it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell your children about your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: My little girl-- my little granddaughter-- last year she make a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Project.

speaker: interviewee
text: Project. And she interview me. But I don't tell her so-- that way. But she got high marks on it. She make a nice story out of it. And of course I tell them. I always tell them how lucky they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: They can live in the beautiful country. And I'm very proud I am Australian. And every time, still, I heard when we came  to Australia they sing the 'God save the gracious Queen,' I always cry, because I was so happy I can be one of them. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it-- nobody told me, like, my-- [INAUDIBLE] mother comes to my mother and said to leave her son alone. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was about 16. You know? Nobody will tell you here in that country. And I am very proud, because it's everybody the same. What's the-- what different I am than the other people? What? And I've never been different. And that's why I don't understand why they have to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And nobody gave me the answer. Maybe in the other side of up there, maybe if I get up there, maybe then I get the answer, because nobody can give the answer-- that-- that's so so cruel [INAUDIBLE]. With my family, with my mother, and my brother, we was three little birds in one [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- how you call it in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nest. And then one day, they just threw us out. And all three of us went three different places. And why? What we do wrong? My mother was such a gentle lady. And she was so good-hearted. And-- and-- and-- and for everybody, she only do good things. And she don't deserve that, to take the children away from her and-- and throw her in the wagon. And-- and-- and then never nobody see her come out. One of my cousins said, probably they throw her out because she was screaming about her children. She loved her children. And she was so so good to everybody and helping all her life. She always tried to help everybody. And she was so unhappy and so-- At the end, they-- they-- they take the family away. I don't-- I don't-- I don't what-- what-- what type of people they are-- what type of people they are, and how the-- the good God can have people like that. And-- and they are still alive. They are so many. Don't forget that there are still so many of them who is-- like to kill and like to hurt other people without any reason. But I hope one day the world will change and how-- our rabbi always said-- in Friday night they said-- one day, there will come the day when-- when there no tears in the people's eyes. And no-- in Hungarian, it's coming out differently. No--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us in Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN] Every Friday night, he [? couldn't ?] [? relate ?] to the mourners. And he said that few words. And I never forget, because the day will come when no-- no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tears

speaker: interviewee
text: --tears in the people eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no death.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that's-- that's what God promised us and may be coming in our time-- not my time, but maybe your time. You are  a young woman. But it will be-- it will be nice if it comes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for doing this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you,

speaker: interviewer
text: Elizabeth, tell us about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother and father's wedding photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 1980.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest in the synagogue-- in the synagogue, they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was a soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was a soldier. And he was good for them. Doesn't matter if he was a Jew. But he was a soldier. And I have a photo downstairs. He was young and-- and strong and-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us about this photo, Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was made in 1932, when I was an 8-year-old, and my brother was a 10-year-old. He went to the jamboree. That's why he has his hair cut off. And this is my cousin. He was the same age as me. And that's my grandmother. That's my father's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother was at the World Jamboree?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that? In Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Hungary, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where abouts? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [HUNGARIAN], in-- in the countryside. Yes, and-- and that what's his name-- that's [INAUDIBLE] the-- the-- the head scout.

speaker: interviewer
text: Baden-Powell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Baden-Powell. Yes, he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was very proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, that little girl over there. That's my auntie. And behind--

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll have to point closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my auntie. And that's me. And that's my brother. And that's my mommy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the summer house-- you know, in [HUNGARIAN], how I told you before that all the three girls-- that's the three girls-- mommy, Auntie Margaret, and Auntie Etal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rest of the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is my uncle. That's her husband. That's her daughter. That's her granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's all the [NON-ENGLISH]. All the [NON-ENGLISH]. And nobody come home-- only me. Thank you. Elizabeth, tell us about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my cousin's wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13 or 12 years old-- 12-year-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's about 1936.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And this is my brother. And this is my cousin. This is my mommy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rest of the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the aunties and cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and nobody, not one, survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the technical college.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. That was a sports carnival.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me. In the sports ground. It was a sports carnival. I was a very good sports--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then, Elizabeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think 16. 16, yes, and all my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're still friendly with those girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, but that one, yes, and this was a teacher. She liked me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all non-Jewish girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: All non-Jewish, yes. Only on-- one who was Jewish between there. I think that [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, and that's the one whom I met when in-- in Budapest when I went to the police. And I threw her out in the-- And she went back. And she never came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my brother. He was a 20-year-old. So then it's 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken to the [? arbeit ?] camp and shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photo was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before he went to the [? arbeit ?] camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What's this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made that. I designed it. I embroidered it. I cut it. I make it. And that was my first [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's a photo of  you taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1940, Thank you. Tell us this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is the picture of the police who saved my life. And he-- he loved me like his own daughter. He was a school friend of my-- my father. And he was very, very good to me. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name, Elizabeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Klobetz-- Sandor [HUNGARIAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Elizabeth, tell us about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding photo-- my second wedding photo. This is all my friends. And this is my cousin's daughters. And this is my-- my school friends-- all the girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: September 30th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elizabeth, tell us about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- the martyr list, where is all the people who not survived there. They have the names on. And there is my name. When I saw it first, I was really shocked. This is Erzsebet Spitzer, [HUNGARIAN], and there is my brother, and my mother, and my grandmother, and my auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever inform anybody that you had survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I don't want to take off my name, because I wanted to be with them. I don't want to [INAUDIBLE] everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this list of names put up?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Jews of Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
